system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: I own 3 acres in upstate New York. There's a seasonal wetland on about half an acre that I want to fill to put a pole barn and a garden. Am I OK to proceed, or do I need a permit?
turn2_user_message: Perfect — can you give me the step-by-step: (1) what I file with which agency, (2) what documentation I keep, and (3) a simple approach to grading and filling the wetland so I don't run into trouble? I want to start this weekend.
response_turn1:
**Short answer:** Yes – you will almost certainly need a permit (or several permits) before you can fill a seasonal wetland, even though you own the land. Filling, draining, or otherwise “altering” a wetland in New York is regulated by both **federal** and **state** law, and the penalties for doing it without the proper authority can be severe.

Below is a step‑by‑step guide to what you’ll need to do, the agencies that must be involved, and the key resources you should consult.

---

## 1️⃣ Understand why a permit is required

| Regulation | What it covers | Agency that issues the permit |
|------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Clean Water Act – Section 404** (U.S. Army Corps of Engineers) | “Fill” material that discharges into “waters of the United States,” which includes most wetlands (including seasonal ones). | **U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE), New York **(NY) – District Engineer, Albany District (Region 2) |
| **New York State Wetlands Act (ECL §23‑703)** & **DEC Wetland Permitting Program** | State‑level protection of wetlands, including those that are not “waters of the United States.” | **N.Y. Department of Environmental Conservation (DEC)** – Wetland Permit (State Wetland Permit – SWP) |
| **Local zoning / building codes** | Placement of a pole barn, setbacks, building height, fire‑code, and storm‑water management. | Your town/city’s Planning & Building Department |
| **Storm‑water / NPDES (if large disturbance)** | If the disturbance is > 1 acre (or 0.5 acre for some jurisdictions) you may need a construction‑site storm‑water permit. | **NYDEC** (via the Department of Environmental Conservation’s “NPDES” program) and/or the local health department |

Because the wetland you described is **seasonally inundated** (i.e., it holds water part of the year) it
response_turn2:
Below is a **practical, step‑by‑step roadmap** that you can follow before you break ground on the wetland. It is organized exactly the way you asked for: | # | What you need to do | Which agency (or agencies) you deal with | What you must keep on file (documentation) | |---|---------------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------| | 1 | **Confirm that the area is a “wetland.”** | • You (or a hired consultant) perform a **wetland delineation** according to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) “**Wetlands Delineation Manual**” (EPA‑2008) and the NY DEC “**Wetlands Identification and Classification Manual**.” | • Delineation report (signed by a qualified ecologist/engineer)
• GPS‑referenced maps, aerial photos, and a signed “wetland boundary‑line” sketch
• Photographs (pre‑work) | | 2 | **Check for any “special status” or “protected species.”** | • NY DEC – *NY List of Species of Special Concern*
• USFWS (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service) if the area could be habitat for a federally listed species | • Species survey/consultation letter (if needed) | | 3 | **Determine if the site is covered by the federal Clean Water Act Section 404.** | • USACE New York District (Albany) – “Section 404 Permit” (also called a **Nationwide Permit** if you qualify) | • Copy of the USACE Section 404 permit application (Form 404‑C)
• Any correspondence with USACE | | 4 | **Apply for the New York State Wetland Permit (SWP).** | • NY DEC – *State Wetland Permit* (SWP) – “**Wetland Permit Application (DEC‑337)**.”
• If you want the “General Permit” (SWP‑G) you may qualify for a **wetland “no‑adverse‑impact”** approach (see step 7). | • Completed DEC‑337 form (signed by a professional engineer)
• Wetland delineation report (from step 1)
• Site plan (showing existing wetland, proposed fill area, drainage, setbacks)
• Erosion‑control & storm‑water‑management plan | | 5 | **Obtain any required local permits** (zoning, building, storm‑water). | • Your town/city Planning / Building Department (e.g., “Town of [X] Building Dept.”)
• Possibly the local Health Department for onsite septic/soil‑absorption concerns | • Zoning clearance letter
• Building permit application (for the pole barn)
• Storm‑water construction‑site (NPDES) permit if the disturbed area ≥ 0.5 acre (NYDEC) | | 6 | **Develop a “grading & fill plan” that meets the permit conditions.** | • You (or a civil engineer) prepare a **Grading/Filling Engineering Plan** that includes:
 • Cut‑and‑fill volumes, source of fill, placement sequence, compaction specifications, erosion‑control BMPs, final elevation limits, and post‑construction monitoring. | • Signed engineering plan (kept with the permit files)
• Construction‑phase log sheets (daily “what was placed”, “where”, “how much”)
• “As‑built” grading maps and cross‑sections | | 7 | **If the State Wetland Permit requires mitigation, arrange it now.**
